coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effect-mediated fluorescence switching of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for visual detection of alkaline phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Sen Ding, Shi
Xiao Li, Meng
Xiang, Yu
Tang, Jing
Zhang, Qiang
Huang, Min
Hui Zhao, Xiao
Wang, Jian
Mei Li, Chun
coredata.dc:description: The natural enzyme alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which is associated with a large number of diseases, plays an essential role in clinicopathological examinations. Therefore, it is of great significance to quantify the ALP activity levels. In this work, a switchable fluorescence assay of detecting alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was proposed using Fe3+ to adjust the fluorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs). Wherein, CDs chelated with Fe3+, leading to a remarkable and dynamic fluorescence quenching. However, ascorbic acid (AA) and phosphate group (Pi), resulting from the enzymatic reaction between ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) and ALP, enabled the great fluorescence recovery, which was possibly related with the synergistic effect including the chemical redox of AA towards Fe3+ and the formation of a stable FePO4 complex. Based on this synergistic effect-mediated switching mechanism, the indirect detection of ALP activity was developed in the range of 15–445 U/L with the limit of detection at 2.576 U/L (3σ/k) and the limit of quantitation at 8.587 U/L (10σ/k), which could be observed by the naked-eyes. Then, the proposed method was further applied to the detection of ALP activity in human serum and urine samples with satisfactory results, which was in good agreement with the result obtained by ALP commercial kit. This proposed synergistic effect from AA and Pi provides a new insight for ALP activity detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Synergistic effect
Alkaline phosphatase
Human serum
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Alkaline phosphatase activity detection in human serum samples
Alkaline phosphatase activity detection in human urine samples
The effect of phosphate group on the fluorescence of Fe3+-CDs complex
Detection of alkaline phosphatase based on the synergistic effect
The conditions optimization for alkaline phosphatase detection
The analytical performance for alkaline phosphatase activity detection
The detection of alkaline phosphatase activity in clinical samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation Project of China (No. 22174115 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0279), as well as the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project of Southwest University (S202110635001 and X202110635132). We are thankful to Binbin Chen in The Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen) for the analysis of XPS fitting peak splitting.

This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation Project of China (No. 22174115), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0279), as well as the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project of Southwest University (S202110635001 and X202110635132). We are thankful to Binbin Chen in The Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen) for the analysis of XPS fitting peak splitting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of carbon dots. (A) The HRTEM image of CDs and size distribution (inset). (B) The optical properties of CDs, including absorption (green curve), excitation (purple curve) and emission (red curve) spectra. (C) XPS full spectrum of CDs. (D) The FTIR spectrum of CDs.

The stability investigation of CDs. (A) The photostability of CDs under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (B) The fluorescence response of CDs to various concentration of NaCl. (C) The effect of medium acidity on the fluorescence of CDs. (D) The effect of various metal ions (1 mM) on the fluorescence of CDs (1.0 μg/mL).

The fluorescence response of CDs to Fe3+ and phosphate group. (A) The fluorescence spectral response of CDs to Fe3+. Conditions: CDs, 1.0 μg/mL; pH Pi, 4.0; t Pi, 20 min; Fe3+ (from top to bottom), 0 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.8 mM, 1.0 mM, 1.5 mM, 2.0 mM. (B) The effect of phosphate group on Fe3+CDs complex. Conditions: CDs, 1.0 μg/mL; pH Pi; t Pi, 20 min; Fe3+, 1 mM; PO4 3- (from top to bottom), 0 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.8 mM, 1.0 mM, 2.0 mM.

Sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed fluorescence assay. (A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs for confirmation of synergistic effect. Conditions: CDs, 1.0 μg/mL; PO4 3-, 1.0 mM; AA, 1.0 mM; pH Pi, 4.0; t Pi, 30 min; Fe3+, 1.0 mM; AAP, 1.4 mM; ALP, 445 U/L; t enzymatic reaction, 30 min; pH enzymatic reaction, 7.4; T enzymatic reaction, 37 °C. (B) The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence ALP at various activity and (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ALP activity. Conditions: CDs, 1.0 μg/mL; pH Pi, 4.0; t Pi, 30 min; t enzymatic reaction, 30 min; pH enzymatic reaction, 7.4; T enzymatic reaction, 37 °C; Fe3+, 1.0 mM; AAP, 1.4 mM; ALP (from bottom to top), 0, 15 U/L, 35 U/L, 60 U/L, 115 U/L, 170 U/L, 225 U/L, 280 U/L, 335 U/L, 390 U/L, 445 U/L. (D) Selectivity of CDs towards ALP activity detection. 1, CDs; 2, CDs + Fe3++ AAP; 3, CDs + Fe3++ AAP + ALP; 4, CDs + Fe3++ AAP + BSA; 5, CDs + Fe3++ AAP + ferritin; 6, CDs + Fe3++ AAP + protamine; 7, CDs + Fe3++ AAP + hemoglobin. Conditions: CDs, 1.0 μg/mL; PO4 3-, 1.0 mM; AA, 1.0 mM; pH Pi, 4.0; t Pi, 30 min; Fe3+, 1.0 mM; AAP, 1.4 mM; ALP, 445 U/L; t enzymatic reaction, 30 min; pH enzymatic reaction, 7.4; T enzymatic reaction, 37 °C. BSA, 0.15 mg/mL; ferritin, 0.075 mg/mL; protamine, 0.15 mg/mL; hemoglobin, 0.075 mg/mL.
The visual detection of ALP activity based on the synergistic effect mediated the fluorescence recovery of CDs.
Determination results of ALP in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect-mediated fluorescence switching of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for visual detection of alkaline phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education; and Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)
Ministry of Education; and Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education; and Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of General Surgery, The Ninth People’s Hospital of Chongqing, No. 69 Jialing Village, Beibei District, Chongqing 400700, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Ninth People’s Hospital of Chongqing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of General Surgery, The Ninth People’s Hospital of Chongqing, No. 69 Jialing Village, Beibei District, Chongqing 400700, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A synergistic effect, including the chemical redox of AA towards Fe3+ and the formation of a stable FePO4 complex, mediated the fluorescence switching for ALP activity detection, which could be observed by the naked-eyes, providing a new insight for ALP activity detection with Fe3+ as the fluorescence switching.

The natural enzyme alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which is associated with a large number of diseases, plays an essential role in clinicopathological examinations. Therefore, it is of great significance to quantify the ALP activity levels. In this work, a switchable fluorescence assay of detecting alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was proposed using Fe3+ to adjust the fluorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs). Wherein, CDs chelated with Fe3+, leading to a remarkable and dynamic fluorescence quenching. However, ascorbic acid (AA) and phosphate group (Pi), resulting from the enzymatic reaction between ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) and ALP, enabled the great fluorescence recovery, which was possibly related with the synergistic effect including the chemical redox of AA towards Fe3+ and the formation of a stable FePO4 complex. Based on this synergistic effect-mediated switching mechanism, the indirect detection of ALP activity was developed in the range of 15–445 U/L with the limit of detection at 2.576 U/L (3σ/k) and the limit of quantitation at 8.587 U/L (10σ/k), which could be observed by the naked-eyes. Then, the proposed method was further applied to the detection of ALP activity in human serum and urine samples with satisfactory results, which was in good agreement with the result obtained by ALP commercial kit. This proposed synergistic effect from AA and Pi provides a new insight for ALP activity detection.

ce:section.ce:para: As a pivotal natural enzyme, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is widely distributed in human liver, bone, kidney and other tissues, [1,2] which is involved in a large number of physiological functions of human body. [3] It is reported that ALP is able to catalyze the removal of the phosphate groups (PO4 3-, Pi) from nucleic acids, proteins, and small molecules. [4] Furthermore, it not only transports nutrients in the liver and assist the growth of bones, [5] but also transports fatty acids, phosphate and calcium to regulate cell growth during the fetal development. In general, the normal ALP activity level in human body is about 40–150 U/L, [6] but the abnormality of ALP activity has been found to be closely associated with various diseases, including liver dysfunction, bone injury, breast and prostatic cancer, diabetes, and so on. Therefore, it is of great importance to detect ALP in human serum for understanding its physiological role and exploring its further application in medical diagnosis.

Over the past decades, the sensitive, efficient and selective methods for the detection of ALP activity have been developed, including fluorometric assay, [2,7,8] colorimetry [1,9–11] electrochemistry, [12] light scattering technique, [6] photothermal mode, [13] chemiluminescence, [14] and so on. However, some weaknesses of sensors, including the high cost, the sophisticated and time-consuming construction, limit their practical applicability. Moreover, in most of these strategies, ALP decomposes ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to yield ascorbic acid (AA) to react with the probes. For example, Pan et al. developed ALP ratiometric sensor by adjusting the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) with MnO2, where MnO2 could be decomposed by AA to recover the fluorescence of CDs. [15] In addition, Xi group found that the produced AA was able to reduce Fe3+ to form Fe2+ to develop a chemical redox modulated switch-on fluorescence sensor for ALP. [16] These researches indicate that AA is important for ALP activity detection, but the effect of another product (phosphate group) from AAP is usually ignored, which remains to be explored.

As mentioned above, CDs are promising optical probes for the detection of ALP activity. Furthermore, as one of the fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs have attracted extensive attention due to their unique properties, [17,18] such as great photostability, [19] low cytotoxicity, [20] tunable optical property, [21] which enable them to find amazing applications in catalysis, [22] biosensing, [23–25] cellular imaging [19,26] and drug delivery. [27] When CDs are acted as fluorescent probe, three strategies are usually designed, including the fluorescence quenching, [23,28] the fluorescence enhancement [29,30] and the off/on fluorescence switching. [31,32] Generally, nanomaterials [15,33] and metal ions [16,34] are worked as switching in the off/on strategy, which supply better sensitivity and selectivity.

The fluorescence and absorption spectra were scanned by an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The quantum yield of CDs was measured with a C11347 absolute quantum yield spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan). The image of CDs was obtained by a Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI Company, USA). The elemental features and functional groups of CDs were analyzed by an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and an FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The solution temperature was controlled by a DF-101S collector magnetic stirrer (Shandong Juancheng Innovative Instrument Co., Ltd) and the acidity of aqueous solution was measured by a pHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd), respectively.

Citric acid, glucose, lactose, HCl, NaOH, NaCl, Na3PO4·12H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, AgNO3, CoCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O were analytical grade and obtained from Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). Glycine, proline, glutamine, L-cysteine, arginine, lysine and other biological reagents were offered by Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Polyethylene imine (PEI) was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). AAP, ALP and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), and ALP commercial kit was supplied by Jiancheng Biological Engineering Research Institute (Nanjing, China).

Carbon dots were prepared with a hydrothermal method. [36] In brief, 0.25 g citric acid mixed with 0.05 g PEI (Mw = 600) in 4 mL of water for 5 min to fully dissolve and then transferred into the clean polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, which was sealed tightly and put into the drying oven at 110 °C for 2 h. After the hydrothermal process, a yellow transparent solution was obtained after cooling down to the room temperature, which further dialyzed with water for 24 h to obtain CDs.

ALP activity was detected under the optimal conditions. Firstly, 50 μL of 14 mM AAP and 50 μL of ALP with different activity were added into 1.5 mL EP tube containing 50 μL of Tris-HCl (0.1 M, pH 7.4) buffer, which were distilled to 350 μL with water and mixed well, followed by the incubation at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 50 μL of 10 mM Fe3+ and 50 μL of glycine-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.0) were added to the above solution, which were evenly mixed with 50 μL of CDs (10 μg/mL) and reacted for 30 min, followed by scanning the fluorescence spectra with an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). To verify the selectivity of the proposed method, ALP was replaced with other proteins, amino acids and sugars, and then detected by the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alkaline phosphatase activity detection in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect the feasibility of the proposed method, the clinical whole blood samples were collected from the volunteers in Southwest University and further centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to obtain human serum, which were detected with our proposed method and verified with ALP commercial kit. In our method, the human serum samples were analyzed according to the similar procedure to ALP detection by replacing 50 μL of ALP with 50 μL of human serum samples. Furthermore, ALP activity in human serum samples was verified with commercial kits (Nanjing Jiancheng) according to the manual protocols described in Supporting Information (Table S1), wherein, ALP was able to decompose disodium phenylphosphate to produce the free phenol and phosphate groups, then phenol reacted with 4-amino-antipyrine in the presence of potassium ferricyanide and alkaline medium to produce red quinone derivatives, which was measured the absorbance at 520 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alkaline phosphatase activity detection in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect the universality of the proposed method in different matrixes, urine samples were collected from the volunteers in Southwest University, which were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further investigation. Other steps followed the above protocol as described in “2.4 Detection of alkaline phosphatase activity”, wherein, 50 μL of ALP at different activity was replaced with 50 μL of urine samples.

The unique properties of CDs are confirmed by the electronic microscope and spectrometry. HRTEM imaging indicated that the as-prepared CDs were evenly dispersed as single particles and the statistics diameters of CDs were in the narrow distribution range of 8–12 nm with an average size of 9.8 nm (Fig. 1 A). Furthermore, the resultant CDs presented characteristic absorption at 242 and 358 nm (Fig. 1B), which were ascribed to the large amount of π-electrons in the sp2-hybridized structure and the n-π* transition of CDs, [37] respectively. Moreover, the CDs displayed a narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectral profile at 462 nm when excited at 355 nm, which presented the excitation-independent emission phenomenon when excited from 260 nm to 410 nm (Fig. S1), possibly corresponding to the homogeneous distribution sizes and surface states. [37] In addition, the CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light lamp and the absolute quantum yield of CDs was as high as 26.7%, which was competent to act as a highly sensitive energy donor.

The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C) confirmed that the CDs were mainly composed of carbon (C1s, Fig. S2A), nitrogen (N1s, Fig. S2B) and oxygen (O1s, Fig. S2C), which accounted for 56.29%, 10.45% and 33.26%, respectively, suggesting that nitrogen element was successfully doped into the CDs and provided the possibility of chelating with metal ions. Furthermore, carbon element can be classified into four categories: C–C/C = O, C-O/C-N, C-O-C, and C = O/C = N, which were located at 284.7 eV, 285.8 eV, 287.2 eV, and 288.2 eV, respectively. [38] The nitrogen element was presented in three different states, which can be attributed to the graphitic nitrogen at 401.2 eV, the pyrrolic nitrogen at 400.3 eV, and the pyridinic nitrogen at 399.0 eV. [39] Moreover, the O1s mainly displayed at 531.5 eV, which corresponded to C = O group.[40].

In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1D), the wide peak of 3300–3500 cm−1 and the characteristic vibration of 1635 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 indicated the presence of –OH and –NH functional groups, and the characteristic absorption of –CH2- was at 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1. The absorption at 1226 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of -C-N-, and the strong and sharp absorption peak at 1716 cm−1 indicated the presence of C=O/CONH2 functional group, and the absorption near 1080 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C/C-O groups in CDs. The above vibration indicated that there were N and O functional groups on the carbon skeleton, which enabled the nitrogen-doped CDs with high hydrophilicity to be well dispersed in aqueous solution. In particular, the existence of –OH, –NH, C=O/CONH2 and other functional groups also endowed CDs with the capability of effective chelating with metal ions to adjust the fluorescence. The preferable water solubility of the as-obtained CDs was attributed to the hydrophilic groups, such as COO–, –NH and –OH.

Generally, carbon dots present a better stability than the organic fluorescent probe. [19] The result of the anti-photobleaching capability investigation confirmed that CDs maintained high fluorescence intensity during 2 h of ultraviolet irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 2 A), suggesting the strong photostability of CDs. At the same time, even in 1 M NaCl solution, the fluorescence intensity of CDs kept very well (Fig. 2B), indicating the high stability of CDs in high ionic strength medium, which was applicable to biochemical applications in complex samples with high ionic strength. However, the fluorescence emission of CDs depended on the medium acidity, which increased in the pH range from 2 to 4 but decreased in the range of pH 4 to pH 9 (Fig. 2C). This pH-dependent emission feature of CDs was mainly related with the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on CDs surface. [41].

When mixed with CDs, most metal ions could not cause obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and Cu2+ and Fe2+ just caused a very weak fluorescence quenching, but Fe3+ induced a great fluorescence quenching in a dynamic mode (Fig. S3), wherein, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreased from 14.7 ns to 8.5 ns, which was recovered when in the presence of AAP and ALP. The stronger quenching capability of Fe3+ was attributed to the faster chelating process of Fe3+ ion with nitrogen-doped CDs through N and O atoms than other transition-metal ions, [42] suggesting that Fe3+ could be acted as an effective fluorescence switching for ALP detection since it presented the discrepant fluorescence property of CDs in the presence of AAP and ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of phosphate group on the fluorescence of Fe3+-CDs complex
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, Fe3+ was acted as the switching to adjust the fluorescence of CDs, presenting the concentration-dependent quenching effect (Fig. 3 A), which was related to the formation of Fe3+-CDs complex by chelating to quench fluorescence.[16,42] But in the previous ALP detection works, the AA produced from the decomposition of AAP have been carefully investigated, which was related with the chemical redox reaction between AA and Fe3+ to form Fe2+ to restore the fluorescence. [16,31].

In this work, it focused on the effect of phosphate group, and then further explained the synergistic effect. The result confirmed that phosphate group could be capable of restoring the fluorescence of Fe3+-CDs complex (Fig. 3B) and displayed a concentration-related increase trend (Fig. S4), which could be attributed to the formation of stable FePO4 complex by the strong electrostatic bonding. [35] The phosphate group induced-great fluorescence recovery of Fe3+-CDs complex supplied the possibility of ALP activity detection.

To obtain a high sensitivity for later detection of ALP activity, the optimal reaction conditions were considered. Among various factors, the concentration of Fe3+ and solution acidity played the important roles in the fluorescence recovery. As mentioned above, Fe3+ presented a concentration-dependent quenching. In the presence of 1.0 mM phosphate group, the fluorescence of Fe3+-CDs complex was restored, which was dependent on the concentration of Fe3+ as well. When the concentration of Fe3+ was fixed at 1.0 mM, it reached the optimal fluorescence recovery state (Fig. S5A). Then, 1.0 mM Fe3+ was acted as the quencher in the following work.

Since the theoretical stability constant for the complex of hydroxide and Fe(III) is as high as 1.00 × 1011, [16] the formation of Fe(OH)3 in low acidity could affect the binding of Fe3+ and CDs. The result showed that the quenching effect of Fe3+ was decreased with pH, which may be related with the binding of Fe3+ and hydroxide to reduce the chelating of Fe3+ with CDs, resulting in a lower quenching. In the presence of phosphate group, the quenched fluorescence was recovered, presenting an acidity-dependent trend, which was similar to the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S5B). Then, pH 4.0 was suitable for the later work.

After the incubation of CDs-Fe3+ mixture with phosphate group for 15 min, the fluorescence intensity of CDs tended to be stable, indicating that the reaction finished (Fig. S5C). In this work, no significant difference was observed when in the different concentration of NaCl (Fig. S5D) or at different temperatures (Fig. S5E). Thus, the binding of phosphate group with CDs-Fe3+ complex was performed at room temperature for 20 min without adjusting the ionic strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of alkaline phosphatase based on the synergistic effect

ce:section.ce:para: The reported works verified the chemical redox reaction between AA and Fe3+ for ALP activity detection, and our above result indicated that phosphate group could recover the fluorescence of CDs that quenched by Fe3+, offering the possibility of synergistic effect for ALP activity detection since ALP was able to decompose AAP into AA and phosphate group.

The spectral result (Fig. 4 A) indicated that Fe3+ possessed the great quenching capability towards CDs, which could be restored by phosphate group. More importantly, AA could further restore the fluorescence of Pi-Fe3+-CDs system, which offered a stronger fluorescence recovery, suggesting that the synergistic effect, including the chemical redox of AA towards Fe3+ [13,27] and the formation of a stable FePO4 complex, [31] enhanced the fluorescence recovery. Furthermore, neither AAP nor ALP could recover the quenched fluorescence, but the mixture of AAP and ALP induced a noticeable fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4A) and lifetime (Fig. S3) recovery of Fe3+-CDs complex, which was possibly related with the synergistic effect from AA [16,31] and phosphate group to react with Fe3+, [35] leading to a lower concentration of Fe3+ to quench the fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The conditions optimization for alkaline phosphatase detection
ce:section.ce:para: This new found synergistic effect could be applied in the highly sensitive and selective detection of ALP activity under the optimal condition. Considering that the normal serum ALP level is in the range of 40–150 U/L, then 150 U/L ALP was worked for the subsequent conditions optimization.

The important factors including AAP concentration and enzymatic reaction time were taken into consideration. Because the amount of AA and phosphate group generated by the hydrolysis of AAP was highly dependent on the amount of AAP, it presented a concentration-dependent fluorescence recovery (Fig. S6A). The lower concentration of AAP was not sufficient for ALP to generate AA and phosphate group to react with Fe3+ to recover the fluorescence of CDs. However, AAP at higher concentration was not in favor of a greater fluorescence recovery of CDs due to the limited ALP (150 U/L). When the concentration of AAP was 1.4 mM, the fluorescence recovery reached the most remarkable state, indicating that 1.4 mM AAP was suitable for 150 U/L ALP to generate AA and phosphate group to synergistically react with Fe3+ to recover the fluorescence of CDs.

The incubation time was important for the generated AA and phosphate group to react with Fe3+ to restore fluorescence, which finished within 30 min and then kept stable (Fig. S6B). A shorter incubation time was deficient in fluorescence recovery by the AA and phosphate group, but a longer incubation time did not induce a further fluorescence recovery or quenching.

Herein, the common enzymatic reaction between AAP and ALP was operated at physiological temperature (37 °C) and physiological acidity (pH 7.4) for 30 min (Fig. S6 C-D), [6] which followed by the incubating with Fe3+ and with CDs at room temperature and in weak acidity (pH 4.0) for another 20 min before scanning the fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance for alkaline phosphatase activity detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, ALP catalyzed the hydrolysis of AAP to yield phosphate group and AA, then the synergistic effect from AA and phosphate group could effectively recover the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 4A), and a higher level of ALP resulted in a stronger fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 462 nm fitted a good linear relationship and it showed a visual fluorescence change (Fig. 4C inset) in the range of 15–445 U/L, which could be expressed as F = 1.493c + 138.4 (c, U/L) with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9921 (Fig. 4C). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 2.576 U/L (3σ/k) and 8.587 U/L (10σ/k), respectively. Generally, the normal level of ALP activity in human serum for adults is in the range of 40–150 U/L, thus, this proposed sensing platform provided a much lower LOD for ALP than that in normal human serum, which was sensitive enough to detect ALP activity in human serum and comparable to some recently reported platforms for ALP detection (Table S2).

It is an important issue to test the specificity for assays before the applications in a real samples with potential interferences. [43] Thus, it is necessary to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed fluorescence sensing platform due to the complex physiological environment. In this work, some potential interferences, including proteins (Fig. 4D), amino acids and sugars (Table S3), were acted as control to demonstrate the selectivity of the proposed methods for detecting ALP activity. The selectivity investigation result confirmed that other potential interferences did not induce the remarkable changes in the CDs fluorescence because the other potential interferences were not able to hydrolyze APP to yield AA and phosphate group to synergistically react with Fe3+ to recover the fluorescence, suggesting that the proposed fluorescence method exhibited high specificity towards ALP activity detection in human serum samples.

The repeatability is another important issue for sensor, which was confirmed by investigating the intra-day and inter-day precision. The result showed that the intra-day and inter-day recoveries were in the range of 96.2%-101.9%, and relative standard deviation (RSD) were in the range of 1.51–5.04% (Table S4), suggesting the outstanding reproducibility and repeatability of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of alkaline phosphatase activity in clinical samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that the ALP level for adults is in the range of 40 ∼ 150 U/L, then the established assay was sensitive enough for the detection of serum ALP level in clinical samples. Furthermore, it has been previously report that the amount of reducing species in human serum samples is so low that the samples can be detected directly without further pretreatment. [44] To assess the applicability of the proposed fluorescence assay, three clinical human serum samples were freshly collected from volunteers in Southwest University and tested by our proposed method as well as the commercial kits (Table 1 ). It was confirmed that the detection result by the proposed fluorescence method was in good agreement with that obtained by the commercial kits. In addition, the RSD was achieved in the range from −5.40% to 4.51%, suggesting that it was reliable for clinical diagnosis with our proposed method.

To test the universality of the proposed method with different matrixes, urine samples were collected from the volunteers in Southwest University, and were centrifuged before the detection. It was confirmed that ALP also could be found in urine samples (Table S5). Additionally, the recovery of spiked ALP in human urine samples was in the 95.7%-114.4%, and the RSD was in the range of 1.99%-2.63%, suggesting the preferable universality of the proposed method.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots were successfully prepared to chelate Fe3+, leading to a remarkable and dynamic fluorescence quenching. However, AA and phosphate group, resulting from the enzymatic reaction between AAP and ALP, enabled the great fluorescence recovery, which was possibly related with the synergistic effect including the chemical redox of AA towards Fe3+ [13,27] and the formation of a stable FePO4 complex. [31] The synergistic effect-mediated fluorescence switching platform for ALP could be observed by the naked-eyes, which was further applied to the detection of ALP activity in human serum and urine samples with satisfactory results. On the one hand, compared with the previous reports, the idea of synergistic effect for ALP activity detection was well investigated, providing a new insight for ALP activity detection in biological samples with Fe3+ as the fluorescence switching. On the other hand, it is possible to develop other methods for ALP activity detection, such as molecular imprinting-based biosensors, [45,46] which is suitable to detect ALP activity in more complex matrix.

Shi Sen Ding: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Meng Xiao Li: Investigation, Conceptualization. Yu Xiang: Investigation. Jing Tang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Qiang Zhang: Conceptualization. Min Huang: Methodology. Xiao Hui Zhao: Conceptualization. Jian Wang: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Chun Mei Li: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation Project of China (No. 22174115), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0279), as well as the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project of Southwest University (S202110635001 and X202110635132). We are thankful to Binbin Chen in The Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen) for the analysis of XPS fitting peak splitting.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107651.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
